blurb: One of the most powerfully fragrant native shrubs on the Front Range — billowy clusters of pure-white four-petaled flowers with citrus-sweet scent blanket the arching branches in early summer.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Clumping; slowly suckering with age — well-behaved, not invasive
seasons: Profuse white fragrant flowers Jun–Jul; lush green foliage summer; good yellow fall color; arching vase-shaped stems provide winter structure
wildlife: White flowers attract native bees, bumble bees, and butterflies; dense branching offers nesting cover for songbirds

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 3, surviving to at least 8,000 ft elevation in Colorado. The Plant Select 'Cheyenne' selection was chosen from a specimen that survived decades of neglect and drought at the Cheyenne Research Station — one of the toughest cultivars available for the Front Range. No winter protection needed.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep). Spring planting before summer heat gives roots the longest establishment window; fall planting works well too if watered through October. Water deeply every 7–10 days the first season to establish, then taper off. Container plants dry out fast and root-bound specimens are prone to stress; best planted in-ground promptly. Needs no special soil preparation beyond improving drainage in heavy clay.[1]
care.sun: Full sun gives the most flowers and the best form. Part shade (3–5 hrs sun) is tolerated and useful on the hot west or south side of a building; flowering is somewhat reduced. Avoid dense shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable to a wide range of soils — sandy loam, clay loam, or dry, gravelly ground. Tolerates the alkaline clay-loam typical of Front Range yards. The main requirement is decent drainage; it does not tolerate waterlogged soil in winter. No amendment is needed on average soil.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate once established — twice-monthly deep watering is usually sufficient after the first season in a typical Front Range yard. More in the first year and in sandy soils. Drought-tolerant, but consistent summer moisture improves flower production. Overwatering causes root problems.[1]
care.spacing: Space 5–8 ft apart; allow for the eventual 5–9 ft height and width. Plant as a specimen, at the back of a mixed border, or as an informal screen or hedge. The arching habit needs room to sweep; don't crowd it.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest from cuttings: softwood cuttings in July–August or hardwood cuttings in December both root readily. For seed, stratify in coarse sand at 41°F (~5°C) for 8 weeks before sowing in spring; germination is moderate. Layering also works — pin a low stem to the ground, sever when rooted. The 'Cheyenne' Plant Select cultivar is widely available at Colorado nurseries.[1][2]
care.bloom: June through early July on the Front Range, with sporadic rebloom into mid-July. Flowers set on the prior year's wood, so timing and method of pruning directly affects next year's display.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little to none needed. A light top-dressing of compost in spring is beneficial on poor soils; skip nitrogen-heavy fertilizer, which produces lush foliage at the expense of flowering.[1]
care.maintenance: Prune immediately after flowering ends (July) — never in fall or spring, which removes next year's flower buds. Remove spent flowering stems back to strong new growth or a main framework branch. Every 5 years, cut the oldest, woodiest canes to the ground in a renewal prune to keep the shrub vigorous and floriferous. The 'Cheyenne' selection stays tidy for many years with minimal pruning.[1]
care.troubles: Generally pest- and disease-free. Aphids occasionally cluster on new growth in spring; blast off with water. Scale insects are rare. Powdery mildew can appear in dense, humid spots — improve airflow by thinning. The most common failure is waterlogged soil or fall pruning that removes next year's flower buds.[1]

edible.summary: Mock orange is an ornamental shrub, not a food plant — no part is eaten, though it is not notably poisonous.[2]
edible.caution: Mock orange is grown purely for its fragrant flowers and garden value; it has no documented edible parts, and despite the citrus-like scent the flowers and fruit are not a substitute for orange or any edible citrus. The dry woody seed capsules and the foliage are not food. There is no significant toxicity reported, but there is also no reason to eat any part of it — treat it as strictly ornamental. (Do not confuse it with unrelated 'mock orange' / Pittosporum or with true citrus.)[2]

references.name: Plant Select — Cheyenne® Mock Orange (Philadelphus lewisii 'PWY01S')
